coredata.dc:title: A novel one-step and green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from saffron for cell imaging and sensing of prilocaine
coredata.dc:creator: Ensafi, Ali A.
Hghighat Sefat, S.
Kazemifard, N.
Rezaei, B.
Moradi, F.
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, a novel and green strategy have been successfully established to synthesize carbon dots from saffron. In this synthesis, a simple method based on hydrothermal treatment was used to synthesis carbon dots with a quantum yield of 23.6%. The as-synthesized carbon dots exhibited many advantages including high fluorescence intensity, water solubility, stability, non-toxic, eco-friendly and importantly using in sensing drugs and cell imaging. Also, the carbon dots were used to assay prilocaine based on quenching assay, without any labeling or using chemical reagents. The influence of several experimental parameters affecting the optical signal, were studied. Under the optimal conditions, prilocaine was measured in a linear range of 2.3–400nmolL−1 (0.5–90ngmL−1). The sensor enables to detect prilocaine as low as 1.8nmolL−1. Finally, the carbon dots were applied for cell imaging of bone marrow cells and Olfactory mucosa cells from rats to illustrate their potential in various applications, with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Saffron
Green synthesis
Carbon dots
Prilocaine detection
Quenching assay
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the prepared carbon dots
Optimization of analytical conditions for the determination of prilocaine
Possible mechanism of the detection of prilocaine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A): Schematic diagram for the synthesis process of the carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment from Saffron; (B): Schematic illustration of the detection principle in the form of a sketch; C): Possible mechanism of detection prilocaine with the carbon dots; (D): Structure of picrocrocin and its decomposing during the saffron drying; (E): Possible mechanism for the formation of the carbon dots.
(A): TEM image of the carbon dots; (B): DLS diagram of the carbon dots; and (C): Zeta potential diagram of the carbon dots.
(A): EDX spectrum of the carbon dots; and B): XRD pattern of the carbon dots.
(A): FT-IR spectrum of the carbon dots; Photograph of the carbon dots under visible light (B); and under UV light at 400nm (C).

(A): UV–vis absorption spectra of the carbon dots and Saffron; (B): The excitation-dependent emission of the carbon dots. (C): Optimization of the concentration of the initial CDs solution; Optimization of the synthesis conditions for the reaction time (D), and for the reaction temperature (E).

Effects of (A): Storage time on the carbon dots fluorescence intensity; (B): Ionic strength on the carbon dots fluorescence intensity; (C): Solution pH on the carbon dots fluorescence intensity; (D): Influence of different solvents on the carbon dots fluorescence intensity; (E) Time-dependence under UV light (λex 400nm and λem 485nm).

(A): Fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots in the presence of different amount of prilocaine; and (B): Calibration graph for the prilocaine determination using the carbon dots system at the optimum conditions; (C): The selectivity of the sensor toward common compounds in human blood; Conditions: pH, 4.0; Carbon dots, 2.27nmolL−1, the concentration of the tested compounds was the same concentration that reported for human blood.

The bright field microphotograph with the carbon dots at 25°C for 24h (λex: 400nm), (A): Fluorescence imaging of the Olfactory mucosa cells of rats labeled; (B): Fluorescence imaging of the Bone marrow cells of rats labeled; and (C): Cytotoxicity testing results via a MTT assay. The values represent percentage cell viability.
Comparison of the new carbon dots based sensor with some reported methods for the determination of prilocaine.

row.entry: GC–MS–electron impact ionization selected ion monitoring
Optical sensor based on MIP on QDs
Optical sensor based on carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test in human plasma samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average values for three determinations ± standard deviations.

head.ce:title: A novel one-step and green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from saffron for cell imaging and sensing of prilocaine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anatomy, Faculty of Medicine, Iran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, a novel and green strategy have been successfully established to synthesize carbon dots from saffron. In this synthesis, a simple method based on hydrothermal treatment was used to synthesis carbon dots with a quantum yield of 23.6%. The as-synthesized carbon dots exhibited many advantages including high fluorescence intensity, water solubility, stability, non-toxic, eco-friendly and importantly using in sensing drugs and cell imaging. Also, the carbon dots were used to assay prilocaine based on quenching assay, without any labeling or using chemical reagents. The influence of several experimental parameters affecting the optical signal, were studied. Under the optimal conditions, prilocaine was measured in a linear range of 2.3–400nmolL−1 (0.5–90ngmL−1). The sensor enables to detect prilocaine as low as 1.8nmolL−1. Finally, the carbon dots were applied for cell imaging of bone marrow cells and Olfactory mucosa cells from rats to illustrate their potential in various applications, with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: In 2004 during the process of electrophoresis, a new class of carbon nanomaterials with the size of less than 10nm was formed that was named carbon dots [1]. In recent years, carbon dots have attracted significant attention by scientists due to its unique chemical and physical properties compared to another quantum dots. Carbon dots have many advantages such as simplicity of production, eco-friendly, low toxicity, biocompatibility, photostability, favorable photoluminescence, and water solubility [2].

Carbon dots have found many applications in the broad range of science fields such as biological sensing, drug delivery, cell imaging, solar cells, and catalysis. As yet, several procedures have been reported for the synthesis of carbon dots such as pyrolysis [3], acidic oxidation [4], laser ablation [5], hydrothermal treatments [6] and microwave/ultrasonic passivation [2]. However, these methods have drawbacks such as the use of toxic chemical reagents, requires high temperature, time-consuming and tedious synthesis steps. These problems have limited the application of these procedures synthesis [7]. For this reason, in recent years new methods based on green synthesis were developed that use various natural materials such as soy milk [6], egg [8], garlic [2], coffee grounds [9], honey [1], and prawn shell [10] as a carbon source for the synthesis of carbon dots.

Prilocaine is a local anesthetic that plays a critical role in dentistry and minor surgery for temporary relief of pain. It is also often combined with lidocaine as a topical preparation for dermal anesthesia. Prilocaine is chemically designated amino amide type, unlike other amide anesthetics, is a secondary amino derivative of toluidine, and extensively metabolized by the liver [11]. Therefore, detection of prilocaine concentration in plasma with fast, sensitive and selective method is critical. Several methods have been used for analysis of prilocaine in different samples including capillary electrophoresis [11], chiral pressure liquid chromatography [12], HPLC with electrochemical detection [13], LC–MS–MS [14], sequential injection chromatography with Franz cell [15], gas chromatography (GC) [16] and GC–MS [17].

In this study, for the first time, a hydrothermal method was developed for the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from Saffron. This approach has many advantages such as economical carbon source, simple synthesis without the addition of any chemical reagents, single step route, hydrophilic and highly stable carbon dots. Additionally, the luminesce of the carbon dots have been also used as a fluorescence probe for cell imaging. Finally, as-synthesized carbon dots were applied for sensitive and selective detection of prilocaine based on a quenching assay. The suggested fluorescent sensor is a simple, rapid, low costly and green method for sensing prilocaine in biological samples.

Pure saffron was purchased from Ghaen Company (Khorasan, Iran). Boric acid, acetic acid, sodium chloride, phosphoric acid, sodium hydroxide, methyl thiazolyl diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), dimethyl foramide (DMF) and prilocaine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Universal buffer (Britton–Robinson buffer) solutions were prepared by mixing of boric acid/acetic acid/phosphoric acid (0.04molL−1 each) that was titrated to the desired pH with 1.0molL−1 NaOH.

A Jasco V-570 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer was used for recording UV–vis spectra. Luminescence spectra were recorded on a Jasco FP-750 spectrofluorometer with slits width of 10nm. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on KBr plates (at 4000–400cm−1) using a Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Dynamic light scattering instrument (Malvern ZEN3600A, UK) was performed for detecting of the prepared carbon dots size distribution and their diameters. The powder of the carbon dots obtained by Freezer dryer (Alpha 1–2 LDplus, Christ-Germany). A Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS, Model ZEN3600 (UK) was used for zeta potential measurement. X-ray diffraction pattern was prepared on a Bruker D8/Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany). Amicon Ultra-4 centrifugal filter unit with 10kDa MW cut-off (Millipore, Madrid, Spain) was used to purify the carbon dots.

In summary for the synthesis of the carbon dot from saffron, first saffron was completely pulverized until a uniformity powder was obtained. Then, 0.50g of the powder was added into 100mL doubly distilled water. The mixture was stirred for 5min; then the obtained mixture was transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200°C for 14h. The product was cooled to room temperature, and the dark brown mixture was filtered with a 0.45μm and a 0.22μm filter membrane, respectively to remove any large particles. Finally, the powder of the carbon dots was obtained by the freeze-dried method to calculate the efficiency and further characterization. The prepared carbon dots were stable for more than 10 months while stored in a dark place at 4°C. Scheme 1 A shows different steps in the green synthesis process of the carbon dots.

The quantum yield of the carbon dots was measured by comparing the integrated prilocaine intensities (excited at 360nm) and the absorbance values of the carbon dots at 360nm with those of the reference quinine sulfate using the following equation [11]:
(1) Qc =Qr.Ic/Ir.Ar/Ac.ɳ2 c/ɳ2 r
Where ‘Q’ is the quantum yield, ‘I’ the intensity of the luminescent spectra, ‘A’ the absorbance at the exited wavelength and ‘ɳ’ the refractive index of the solvent used; using quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54) in 0.1molL−1 H2SO4 solution as a reference solution (η=1.33), and carbon dots were dissolved in ultrapure water (η=1.33). The subscripts ‘c’ for the carbon dots and ‘r’ for the reference are used in this equation.

The sensing of prilocaine was carried out in the universal buffer (Britton–Robinson buffer) (0.04molL−1, pH 4.0) at room temperature. In a typical analysis, 50μL carbon dots (25.0mgmL−1) suspension was diluted to 3.0mL with the universal buffer solution (pH 4.0). Then, different concentration of prilocaine was added to the carbon dots and mixed thoroughly before measurement. After 2min, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the range of 410–650nm while the excitation wavelength was set at 400nm. For quantitative measurement, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture at 485nm (maximum emission wavelength) was measured in the absence and presence of prilocaine and recorded as F0 and F, respectively. F0–F was calculated as a response function, to evaluate the prilocaine concentration. Scheme 1B shows the detection principle of the proposed sensor.

Plasma samples (obtained from the Health Center of the Isfahan University of Technology) were stored frozen until the assay. To precipitate any proteins, 5.0mL of the plasma sample plus 5.0mL of 10% (w/v) tricholoroacetic acid was transferred into a centrifuge tube and mixed well, and then it was centrifuged for 10min at 10,000rpm. The clear top phase was filtered through a 0.45-μm Millipore filter and ten times were diluted with the universal buffer (pH 4.0) before analysis.

For the analysis, different volumes of the standard prilocaine solution were spiked into the diluted plasma samples. Then, 1.0mL of the mixture was transferred into a cell containing 50μL of the carbon dots (0.25mgmL−1) and 2.0mL of the universal buffer solution (pH 4.0). The fluorescence intensity of the mixture (at 485nm) was measured using standard addition method.

For cell imaging, two types of important rat’s cells; Olfactory mucosa cells and Bone marrow cells were grown in a culture media. For bioimaging, the filtered carbon dots solution (0.25mgmL−1, 30–40μL) was mixed with grown rat’s cells and kept at 25°C for 24h. Then, the cells were washed with ultrapure water to remove excess carbon dots and then centrifuged at 3000rpm for 15min. Finally, the fluorescence imaging of the cell was recorded using a fluorescence microscope. (IX80, Olympus Corp., Tokyo, Japan, λex =400nm).

Methyl thiazolyl diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the CDs. Cellular toxicity was determined by measuring the activity of mitochondrial enzymes in live cells to transform the soluble yellow MTT solution to an insoluble purple formazan product. The cells (Olfactory mucosa cells and Bone marrow cells) were treated with different doses of the CDs solutions and incubation them for 24h. Then, the cells attached to each plate were treated with 50μL of freshly prepared MTT solution (5mgmL−1) and were incubated again for 4h to allow forming of violet colored formazan. Next, the supernatant was carefully removed and the formazan was dispersed in a DMF solution (50% aqueous). Multimode microplate reader was use to read the absorbance at 570nm. The untreated cells were used as a control for calculating the relative percentage cell viability using the following equation [18,19]:
(2) %Cellviability=(A570 intreatedsample/A570 incontrolsample)×100%
Here A570 refers to the absorbance at 570nm.

Saffron uses as a seasoning and coloring agent in food. Saffron stigma is a diversified chemical compound such as carbohydrates, minerals (like copper, potassium, calcium, manganese, iron, selenium, zinc), mucilage, vitamins (especially thiamine and riboflavin), pigments contains crocin, anthocyanins, carotene, lycopene, zeaxanthin, a fragrant essential oil called safranal, and flavoring agents (picrocrocin). The present chemical compounds in saffron are safe for human and environment [20]. For this reason, saffron was chosen as a safe carbon source.

A significant part of saffron is picrocrocin. As shown in Scheme 1D, picrocrocin consists of two parts, which decompose into D-glucose and free safranal molecule by heating, during the drying of saffron [20,21]. As mentioned, for the synthesis, dried stigma saffron was used as a carbon source, which contains a lot of D-glucose and another carbohydrate. There is no precise mechanism for this reaction, but the probable path for the formation of carbon dots is from carbon precursors. A possible mechanism for the formation of carbon dots is based on carbonization of the carbohydrates [22]. At first different carbohydrates hydrolysis to form simply carbohydrates like glucose and fructose. Then, dehydration and decomposition of fructose or glucose give rise to different soluble products such as furfural compounds and several organic acids like acetic, formic acid, etc., which act as acid resources for different acid catalytic reactions. The polymerisation and condensation of these products give rise to soluble polymers. The aromatization and carbonization then takes place via condensation and cycloaddition reactions. The result of all these steps leads to the formation of aromatic clusters at a critical concentration. So finally, a possible explosion was occurred to destroy the clusters and form carbon dots (Scheme 1E) [22,23].

TEM was used to identify the particles size and morphology of the as-synthesis carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the shape of the prepared carbon dots is spherical, and as can be seen in this figure, the average size of the carbon dots was estimated as 6nm. The particles are monodisperse confirmed that there is no agglomerated in them. Moreover, the results of dynamic light scattering technique (DLS, Fig. 1B) show a narrow size distribution ranging from 3.5nm to 7.5nm in the prepared carbon dots, which the results are consistent with the TEM results. Also, as shown in Fig. 1C, the zeta potential of CDs was calculated as ∼–20mV.

EDX technique was used to determine the composition of the carbon dots and estimate the abundance of present carbon and oxygen on the carbon dots. Fig. 2 A shows the presence of 68.5% carbon, 24.5% oxygen and the remaining 7% represents potassium and another mineral compound that not decompose during of the synthesis process. A significant proportion of the mineral compounds in saffron is potassium that EDX result shows the possibility of its presence in the as-synthesis CDs [21].
The XRD pattern of carbon dots, in the 10–60° 2θ range, is shown in Fig. 2B. The broad diffraction peaks at about 2θ=23° showed the characteristic graphitic plates of (002).

Fig. 3 A shows the FT-IR spectra of the prepared carbon dots. In this figure, strong and broad peaks between 3300–3500cm−1, from the OH or NH vibrational stretching, could be observed. The small band at 2925cm−1 could be identified as the stretching vibrations of CH bonds. The band at 1772cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and the band at 1672cm−1 appointed to the stretching vibrations of CO or NH. The band at 1454cm−1 could be identified as CN, NH, and COO groups. The band at 1384cm−1 is attributed to the bending of CH and NH and the broad band around 1100–1200cm−1 is ascribed to the bending of CH. The yield, determined by the freeze-dried method, was calculated as 0.25g approximate 50% of the mass yield. The quantum yield, was measured using quinine sulfate as a standard sample, was 23.6%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the prepared carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3B, a solution of carbon dots in visible light was clear yellow and under UV light (400nm) exhibited a bright green-blue fluorescence (Fig. 3C).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the carbon dots showed a sharp peak at 275nm, as showed in Fig. 4 A, which was different from freshly saffron solution. There are three kinds of fluorescent CDs including graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs) and polymer dots (PDs) [24]. In all three types, the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a strong peak in the range of 230–320nm, with a tail spreading into the visible range. This peak is probably originated from the π-π* transition of the conjugated CC bond and n–π* transition of the CO [24]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs is different to that of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) because the origin of UV–vis absorption in these two fluorescent nanoparticles is different. Therefore, the synthesized nanoparticles in this research are carbon dots, and according to the TEM, these CDs are carbon nanodots [24].

The maximum excitation and emission peaks of the prepared carbon dots were located at 400 and 485nm, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence spectrum with excitation wavelength of 400nm (in addition to having a good appearance and symmetry) has an acceptable fluorescence intensity in this λmax. So, 400nm was chosen as an excitation wavelength and 485nm as an emission wavelength that having highest fluorescence intensity (λmax) for further experiments. Although the best fluorescence spectrum of the CDs was obtained at λex of 400nm, freshly saffron solution did not show any fluorescence emission at this λex. This reflects the importance of the hydrothermal method in the preparation of the carbon dots. Like another carbon dots, the emission peak of the prepared carbon dots depends on the excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 4B, the emission peak has red-shifted with increasing in the λex from 340 to 440nm. This behavior is due to the different sizes of nanoparticles and a distribution of the various organic groups on the carbon dots surface.

Synthesis conditions for preparation of the carbon dot were optimized. As shown in Fig. 4C, different concentration of saffron was used for this study. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity increases with increasing the concentration of the initial solution and finally, 5mgmL−1 of saffron powder in pure water was chosen as an optimum concentration. Then as shown in Fig. 4D and E, the influence of different reaction times and temperatures were studied for the preparation of the carbon dot. The results showed that with increasing the reaction temperature and reaction time, the fluorescence intensity increased. On the other hand, full width at the half maximum (FWHM) was increased too. In addition, based on the obtained results, 14h and 200°C were selected as reaction time and reaction temperature, respectively.

One of the most important properties of the as-synthesized carbon dots is stability in different conditions. Fig. 5 A confirms that the carbon dots were stable more than 10 months in dark place at 4°C and stable 3 months at room temperature. In addition, the prepared carbon dots demonstrated good stability against freezing and heating. Furthermore, the stability of the carbon dots was studied under the experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5B, the influence of ionic strength on the carbon dots fluorescence were tested by using different concentration of NaCl. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots remained stable in the presence of different ionic strengths. Moreover, the effects of pH and organic solvent on the carbon dots response were studied. As shown in Fig. 5C, change in the solution pH (in the range of 2.0–12.0) has very little change in the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots. As can be seen in Fig. 5D, the fluorescence intensity is almost constant in different polar solvents at high concentration. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity is constant in different nonpolar solvents at low concentration (10μL of CDs in 0.5mL of solvent). Only a minor change in fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots can be seen under UV-light after 10h (Fig. 5E). All the features listed represent a successful synthesis of new type of green and economically carbon dots, which has wide application in various fields and under different conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of analytical conditions for the determination of prilocaine
ce:section.ce:para: Effective parameters on the response of the carbon dots toward prilocaine including sample solution pH, amount of the carbon dots and the reaction time were optimized. First, different solution pHs were examined on the fluorescence intensity. Based on the obtained results in the Supplementary information (Fig. S-1, A), prilocaine had different effects on the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots at various pHs. The maximum response of the carbon dots to prilocaine was achieved at pH 4.0. Therefore, pH 4.0 was chosen as the working pH through all further experiments.

The amount of the carbon dots on the sensor sensitivity toward prilocaine was optimized, and the best response of the carbon dots to prilocaine was observed in 50μL of the carbon dots (0.25mgmL−1) in the final volume of 3.0mL at pH 4.0 (Fig. S-1, B). As shown in the Supplementary information, the fluorescence intensity for higher amounts of the carbon dots is greater than that the spectrofluorimeter can be recorded. The sensitivity of the method decreased with decreasing the quantities of the carbon dots since the signal to noise ratio was also very low.

Furthermore, toward prilocaine determination, the reaction time was optimized too. As shown in Fig. S-1, C (Supplementary information), the interaction between the prilocaine and the carbon dots was happening very fast, and it was reached to equilibrium less than 3min. Therefore, 3.0min was chosen as an optimum time. From the obtained results, pH 4.0, 0.25mgmL−1 carbon dots and a reaction time of 3.0min were selected as the optimal conditions for prilocaine determination.

Under the optimal conditions described above, the fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots in the presence of prilocaine were recorded. As can be seen in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots decreases when the concentration of prilocaine increases. The response function values (F0–F) were obtained with various prilocaine concentrations. A linear range from 0.5ngmL−1 to 90ngmL−1 (2.3–400nmolL−1) was obtained for prilocaine analysis with a regression equation of (F0–F)=1.142Cprilocaine +31.28 (R2 =0.994), as shown in Fig. 6B. The limit of detection was calculated from 3s/m (where “s” is the standard deviation of the blank, with 10 replicates measurements) and m is the slope of the calibration curve. A detection limit of 1.8nmolL−1 prilocaine was obtained.

Additionally, relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.4% and 2.1% were calculated for five repeated measurements of 1.36×10−8 molL−1 (3.0ngmL−1) and 3.1×10−7 molL−1 (70.0ngmL−1) prilocaine, respectively. Moreover, the detection limit and dynamic range of the proposed sensor was comparable with the other reported methods [12,14,25–30] in the literature for measuring prilocaine (Table 1 ).

For investigation of the method selectivity, the response of the sensor in the absence and presence of different species such as tryptophane, methionine, glucose, dopamine, and KCl were recorded (all at the same concentrations) in the presence of 2.3nmolL−1 prilocaine. The obtained results are shown in Fig. 6C. As indicated in this figure the response of the sensor is highly selective and can be said that annoying have little effect on the response of the sensor.

After reviewing the sensitivity and selectivity of this analytical strategy, the ability of the carbon dots for prilocaine determination in blood plasma samples was studied. For this purpose, different concentrations of the drug were spiked in four plasma blood samples. Then, the amounts of prilocaine were measured using the recommended procedure. As shown in Table 2 , the precision of this analytical strategy is acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the detection of prilocaine
ce:section.ce:para: According to the structure of prilocaine (Scheme 1B) and the functional groups on the surface of the CDs that was confirmed by the infrared spectroscopy, there is the possibility of forming a hydrogen bond between prilocaine and CDs (as seen in the Scheme 1C). After the formation of hydrogen bonds and thereby reducing the distance between the CDs and prilocaine, the electron transfer is facilitated and consequently, fluorescence quenching has happened.

Study on the living cell is one of the most important fields in the research world. This technique uses to study many diseases and cancer cells. In cell imaging, fluorescence probe uses to show live cells, where many of these probes are toxic and causing cell death or have a weak fluorescence intensities and therefore they must be used at high concentration. In this study, the synthesized carbon dots were used as a probe without any toxic properties in cell imaging (Fig. 7 A and B). The results revealed that the photoluminescence of the carbon dots after 24h penetrates into the cell membrane and cytoplasmic area of the cells, without any damaging the cell nucleus. For this reason, the cells remain alive and can be easily imaged them.

MTT based assay was used for the cytotoxicity study that relies on the color change of MTT by mitochondrial succinate dehydrogenase [31]. The results showed that CDs are low toxic at doses substantially higher than the usual concentrations (<1mgmL−1) required for cell labeling studies (Fig. 7C). In contrast, presumably the prepared carbon dots do not impose any significant toxicity to cells. Moreover, as the images show a low concentration of them creates intense fluorescence intensity. Hence, it is safe for human cell imaging in-vitro and in-vivo applications.

In summary, novel and economical carbon dots were successfully synthesized using hydrothermal treatment of saffron. The fluorescence property of the carbon dots was employed for the easy assaying of prilocaine and importantly in human plasma. The results showed that they are successfully able to sense prilocaine at a minimum level of 0.5ngmL−1. These carbon dots exhibited proper role in sensing and cell imaging without any further modification. The as-synthesis carbon dots were used for cell imaging of two important types of cell, and the excellent results showed that the carbon dots have the best candidate for bioimaging, particularly cancer cells. Overall, all the results demonstrate broaden potential way in bio-imaging, bio-sensing, and other purposes by using nanoparticles, which were synthesis from natural material instead of using the chemical and hazardous material. This helps scientists to protect the environment by developing green chemistry. This work demonstrated the wide linear range and low detection limit of the prepared fluorescent sensor based on the green synthesis carbon dots.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel and green synthesis of high-fluorescent carbon dots originated from honey for sensing and imaging
Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
One-pot green synthesis of optically pH-sensitive carbon dots with upconversion luminescence
Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser irradiation
Sodium hydroxide-mediated hydrogel of citrus pectin for preparation of fluorescent carbon dots for bioimaging
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Fluorescent quantum dots: synthesis, biomedical optical imaging, and biosafety assessment
Synthesis and analytical applications of photoluminescent carbon nanodots
Green synthesis of carbon dots from prawn shells for highly selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Stereoselective determination of R-(−)- and S-(+)-prilocaine in human serum by capillary electrophoresis using a derivatized cyclodextrin and ultraviolet detection
Stereoselective determination of R(−)- and S(+)-prilocaine in human serum using a brush-type chiral stationary phase, solid-phase extraction and UV detection
Determination of prilocaine in human plasma samples using high-performance liquid chromatography with dual-electrode electrochemical detection
Simultaneous determination of bupivacaine mepivacain, prilocaine and ropivacain in human serum by liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry
Automation of simultaneous release tests of two substances by sequential injection chromatography coupled with Franz cell
GC determination of prilocaine HCl in human plasma: analytical application to real samples
Simple analysis of local anaesthetics in human blood using headspace solid-phase microextraction and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry–electron impact ionization selected ion monitoring
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
An overview on saffron, phytochemicals, and medicinal properties
Simple one-step synthesis of highly luminescent carbon dots from orange juice: application as excellent bio-imaging agents
Morphological and structural differences between glucose, cellulose and lignocellulosic biomass derived hydrothermal carbons
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Determination of prilocaine HCl in pharmaceutical preparations by GC–MS method
Determination of prilocaine HCL in pharmaceutical preparation and human plasma with spectrophotometrey
Development and validation of gas chromatography–mass spectroscopy method for determination of prilocaine HCl in human plasma using internal standard methodology
New adsorptive square wave method for trace determination of prilocain in the flow injection system by a fast Fourier analysis
Simple analysis of local anaesthetics in human blood using headspace solid-phase microextraction and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry–electron impact ionization selected ion monitoring
Development of a selective prilocaine optical sensor based on molecularly imprinted shell on CdTe quantum dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ali A. Ensafi was graduated in Chemistry (MSc) from Shiraz University (Iran) in 1988, and he received his Ph.D. in 1991 in Analytical Chemistry at the same University. Then, he joined the Department of Chemistry at the Isfahan University of Technology (Iran). He became full professor in 2001. His research interest comprises the development of new electrochemical and/or chromogenic chemosensors, molecular probes and biosensors for anions, cations, pharmaceutical compounds, and DNA damage. Prof. Ensafi pioneered a new genera tion of DNA-biosensors for detection of DNA damage and awarded three International Awards.

Sahar Hghighat Sefat received her B.Sc. degree in Chemistry in 2013 from Shiraz University of (Iran) and M.Sc. in Analytical Chemistry in 2016 from Isfahan University of Technology. Her research interest is optical and biosensors.

Nafiseh Kazemifard received her B.Sc. degree in Applied Chemistry in 2010 from Kashan University (Iran) and M.Sc. in Analytical Chemistry in 2012 from Esfahan University. She has received her Ph.D. in Analytical Chemistry in the Isfahan University of Technology in 2017. Her research interest is optical and biosensors.
Fatemeh Moradi is a M.D. student in Department of Anatomy, Faculty of Medicine, Iran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran. His research interest in medical sciences.

Behzad Rezaei is Professor of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology. He received his BSc in chemistry from Isfahan University in 1988, his MSc in Analytical Chemistry from Isfahan University in 1991, and his Ph.D. from Isfahan University of Technology in Analytical Chemistry in 1999. His research interests cover electrochemical, flow injection method of analysis and electrochemical sensors.
